question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker (any EOA) submit a validly-signed Authorization where authorization.authorizer has NOT yet called midnight.setIsAuthorized(address(ecrecoverAuthorizer), true, authorizer), causing the downstream IMidnight.setIsAuthorized call to revert after the nonce has already been incremented, leaving the EcrecoverAuthorizer nonce permanently advanced while Midnight isAuthorized state is unchanged? Preconditions: authorizer has never authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight; attacker holds a valid signed Authorization with nonce==0. Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) -> nonce[authorizer]++ -> IMidnight.setIsAuthorized reverts -> transaction reverts -> nonce is NOT incremented (revert unwinds). Invariant tested: nonce must only increment on successful state change in Midnight. Scoped impact: state inconsistency between EcrecoverAuthorizer nonce and Midnight isAuthorized. Proof idea: unit test asserting nonce stays at 0 when Midnight call reverts; fuzz over authorizer addresses that have not pre-authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.touchMarket] Can a market creator exploit the window between setDefaultSettlementFee and touchMarket to create a market with stale default fees, causing a permanent divergence between defaultSettlementFeeCbp[loanToken] and marketState[id].settlementFeeCbp*, where the market fees are lower than the current default? Preconditions: feeSetter sets defaultSettlementFee to X; attacker signs Authorization granting themselves feeSetter-equivalent access; feeSetter then raises default to Y; attacker's authorization is submitted and market is created. Call sequence: setDefaultSettlementFee(token, i, X) -> [attacker submits auth] -> setDefaultSettlementFee(token, i, Y) -> touchMarket(market) -> assert marketSettlementFee != defaultSettlementFee. Invariant tested: newly created market fees must equal current default fees at creation time. Scoped impact: market fee inconsistency with default. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting marketSettlementFee(id, i) == defaultSettlementFee(loanToken, i) immediately after touchMarket.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized delegate (isAuthorized[authorizer][delegate] == true) sign an Authorization on behalf of authorizer, and if the authorizer revokes the delegate's Midnight authorization AFTER the signature is created but BEFORE submission, does the EcrecoverAuthorizer correctly reject the submission? Preconditions: authorizer grants delegate via midnight.setIsAuthorized; delegate signs Authorization; authorizer revokes delegate. Call sequence: midnight.setIsAuthorized(delegate, true, authorizer) -> delegate signs auth -> midnight.setIsAuthorized(delegate, false, authorizer) -> ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, delegateSig). Invariant tested: revoked delegates must not be able to submit previously-signed authorizations. Scoped impact: gate bypass allowing stale delegate to modify isAuthorized state. Proof idea: unit test asserting Unauthorized revert after revocation; fuzz over revocation timing.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker construct an Authorization where authorization.authorizer == authorization.authorized (self-authorization), consuming a nonce without any meaningful state change in Midnight, and does this create a denial-of-service on future nonce-dependent operations? Preconditions: authorizer has authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight; attacker is the authorizer. Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized({authorizer: A, authorized: A, isAuthorized: true}, sig) -> nonce[A]++ -> midnight.isAuthorized[A][A] = true. Invariant tested: self-authorization must not consume nonces or create unexpected isAuthorized state. Scoped impact: accounting corruption of nonce state; potential DoS on future authorizations. Proof idea: unit test asserting nonce increments and midnight.isAuthorized[A][A] becomes true; check if this breaks downstream ratifier checks.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit the same signed Authorization twice (replay) by exploiting any path that does not increment the nonce, given that nonce[authorizer]++ happens before signature verification in the code? Preconditions: valid signed Authorization with nonce N exists. Call sequence: first submission -> nonce becomes N+1 -> second submission with same nonce N -> InvalidNonce revert. Invariant tested: each signed Authorization must be usable exactly once. Scoped impact: replay protection correctness. Proof idea: unit test submitting same auth twice; assert second call reverts with InvalidNonce; fuzz over nonce values.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.multicall] Can an attacker use Midnight.multicall to atomically (1) grant EcrecoverAuthorizer permission, (2) submit a pre-signed Authorization via ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, and (3) revoke EcrecoverAuthorizer permission, leaving Midnight isAuthorized state changed while the EcrecoverAuthorizer permission is revoked, creating an inconsistency? Preconditions: attacker controls authorizer account; has pre-signed Authorization. Call sequence: multicall([setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, true, authorizer), <external call to ecrecoverAuthorizer>, setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, false, authorizer)]). Note: multicall uses delegatecall so external calls to ecrecoverAuthorizer are not possible within multicall. Invariant tested: multicall must not enable atomic grant-use-revoke patterns that bypass authorization intent. Scoped impact: gate bypass. Proof idea: attempt multicall sequence; verify it reverts or succeeds only with correct authorization.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setDefaultSettlementFee + Midnight.touchMarket] Can an unprivileged market creator create a market for a loanToken where defaultSettlementFeeCbp[loanToken] is all zeros (never set by feeSetter), resulting in a market with zero settlement fees at all breakpoints, and does this create a state inconsistency where the market has fees that differ from any future default set for that token? Preconditions: feeSetter has never called setDefaultSettlementFee for loanToken; any user calls touchMarket. Call sequence: touchMarket(market) -> marketState[id].settlementFeeCbp* = 0 -> feeSetter calls setDefaultSettlementFee(loanToken, i, X) -> assert marketSettlementFee(id, i) == 0 != X. Invariant tested: market fees must reflect the default at creation time; post-creation default changes must not retroactively affect existing markets. Scoped impact: state inconsistency between default and market fees; zero-fee market enables fee-free trading. Proof idea: fuzz over loanToken addresses never configured; assert all 7 breakpoints are zero.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketSettlementFee] Can the feeSetter call setMarketSettlementFee on a market ID that corresponds to an uncreated market (tickSpacing == 0), and does the MarketNotCreated check correctly prevent this, or is there a path where the check is bypassed? Preconditions: market with given id has never been touched (tickSpacing == 0). Call sequence: feeSetter calls setMarketSettlementFee(id, 0, fee) -> require(_marketState.tickSpacing > 0) -> should revert. Invariant tested: settlement fee setter must only operate on created markets. Scoped impact: state inconsistency where uncreated market has non-zero fee state. Proof idea: unit test asserting MarketNotCreated revert; fuzz over arbitrary bytes32 ids.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setDefaultContinuousFee + Midnight.touchMarket] Can the feeSetter set defaultContinuousFee[loanToken] to MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE, then a market creator calls touchMarket, and the market inherits MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE, but if feeSetter then sets defaultContinuousFee to 0, does the existing market's continuousFee remain at MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE while new markets for the same loanToken get 0, creating a permanent fee inconsistency between markets? Preconditions: feeSetter sets default to MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE; market A is created; feeSetter sets default to 0; market B is created. Call sequence: setDefaultContinuousFee(token, MAX) -> touchMarket(marketA) -> setDefaultContinuousFee(token, 0) -> touchMarket(marketB). Invariant tested: per-market continuousFee is fixed at creation and independent of subsequent default changes. Scoped impact: state inconsistency between market A and market B fees for same loanToken. Proof idea: differential test asserting continuousFee(idA) == MAX and continuousFee(idB) == 0.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with deadline == block.timestamp (boundary condition), and does the check require(block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline) correctly allow this, potentially enabling a signature that was intended to expire at the current block to still be used? Preconditions: signed Authorization with deadline == block.timestamp. Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) where auth.deadline == block.timestamp. Invariant tested: deadline boundary must be consistently enforced (inclusive vs exclusive). Scoped impact: stale authorization submission at exact deadline boundary. Proof idea: unit test with deadline == block.timestamp asserting success; test with deadline == block.timestamp - 1 asserting Expired revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer uses block.chainid (live) for the domain separator while Midnight uses INITIAL_CHAIN_ID (captured at construction) for market IDs, creating a cross-chain inconsistency where a signature valid on a forked chain can authorize operations on markets whose IDs were computed with the pre-fork chain ID? Preconditions: hard fork changes block.chainid; attacker has a pre-fork signed Authorization. Call sequence: vm.chainId(newChainId) -> ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) -> domain separator changes -> old sig invalid. Invariant tested: authorization domain must be consistent with market ID domain. Scoped impact: state inconsistency between authorization validity and market ID space after hard fork. Proof idea: differential test with chainId change asserting old signatures are rejected.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setRoleSetter + EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who has been granted authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer use that authorization to call setIsAuthorized(newRoleSetter, true, currentRoleSetter) in Midnight, and then use the newRoleSetter to change feeSetter/feeClaimer/tickSpacingSetter, creating a role-controlled parameter inconsistency? Preconditions: attacker obtains isAuthorized[roleSetter][attacker] == true via EcrecoverAuthorizer; roleSetter has authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer. Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized({authorizer: roleSetter, authorized: attacker, isAuthorized: true}) -> attacker calls midnight.setRoleSetter(attacker) -> attacker calls midnight.setFeeSetter(attacker). Invariant tested: role setter operations must only be callable by the designated roleSetter. Scoped impact: role-controlled parameter takeover enabling fee manipulation. Proof idea: unit test asserting setRoleSetter requires msg.sender == roleSetter, not isAuthorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketContinuousFee] Can the feeSetter call setMarketContinuousFee on a market that exists (tickSpacing > 0) while lenders have active positions, causing the continuousFee to change mid-position, and does the existing lenders' pendingFee remain fixed (as documented) while new lenders get the new fee, creating an inconsistency between old and new lender fee obligations in the same market? Preconditions: market created with continuousFee = F1; lender A has credit; feeSetter changes continuousFee to F2; lender B takes a new position. Call sequence: touchMarket -> take (lender A gets credit) -> setMarketContinuousFee(id, F2) -> take (lender B gets credit). Invariant tested: pendingFee for existing lenders must not change when continuousFee changes. Scoped impact: fee accounting inconsistency between lenders in same market. Proof idea: invariant test asserting lender A's pendingFee is unchanged after fee change.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorized == address(0), causing midnight.isAuthorized[authorizer][address(0)] = true, and does this create any exploitable state given that address(0) cannot call functions? Preconditions: authorizer has authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer; attacker signs auth with authorized == address(0). Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized({authorized: address(0), isAuthorized: true}) -> midnight.isAuthorized[authorizer][address(0)] = true. Invariant tested: address(0) must not be authorizable as it cannot initiate calls. Scoped impact: state inconsistency in isAuthorized mapping; potential interaction with zero-address checks. Proof idea: unit test asserting isAuthorized[A][address(0)] can be set; verify no downstream exploit path.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketTickSpacing + EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who obtains tickSpacingSetter authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer call setMarketTickSpacing to reduce tick spacing on a created market, enabling previously inaccessible ticks, and does this create a state inconsistency where existing offers at those ticks (which were previously inaccessible) become fillable without maker consent? Preconditions: market created with DEFAULT_TICK_SPACING=4; offers exist at tick % 4 != 0 but tick % 2 == 0; attacker obtains tickSpacingSetter role via authorization chain. Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized({authorized: attacker, isAuthorized: true}) -> attacker calls setMarketTickSpacing(id, 2) -> previously inaccessible offers become fillable. Invariant tested: tick spacing reduction must not retroactively enable offers that makers did not intend to be fillable. Scoped impact: offer bug enabling fills at unintended ticks. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting offers at tick=2 are fillable after spacing reduction.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.claimSettlementFee + EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who obtains feeClaimer authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer call claimSettlementFee to drain claimableSettlementFee[token] to an attacker-controlled receiver, and does the role change (setFeeClaimer) cause the old feeClaimer to lose access to unclaimed fees, creating a fund freeze? Preconditions: feeClaimer has accumulated claimableSettlementFee; roleSetter authorizes ecrecoverAuthorizer; attacker signs auth to become feeClaimer. Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized({authorized: attacker}) -> attacker calls midnight.setFeeClaimer(attacker) [requires roleSetter role] -> attacker calls claimSettlementFee. Invariant tested: only designated feeClaimer can claim fees; role change must not enable unauthorized fee extraction. Scoped impact: direct loss of settlement fees to unauthorized receiver. Proof idea: unit test asserting claimSettlementFee reverts for non-feeClaimer.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker front-run a legitimate Authorization submission by submitting the same signed message first (since submission is permissionless), consuming the nonce, and causing the legitimate user's submission to fail with InvalidNonce, effectively blocking the intended authorization? Preconditions: victim has signed an Authorization and broadcast it; attacker observes the transaction in mempool. Call sequence: attacker submits ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) first -> nonce incremented -> victim's submission reverts with InvalidNonce. Invariant tested: permissionless submission must not enable front-running DoS on authorization. Scoped impact: fund freeze / DoS on authorization flow. Proof idea: unit test simulating front-run; assert victim's tx reverts; note this is by-design but verify impact.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with isAuthorized == false to revoke an authorization that the authorizer did NOT intend to revoke, if the attacker has obtained a signed Authorization with isAuthorized == false (e.g., from a previous revocation attempt that was never submitted)? Preconditions: authorizer signed auth with isAuthorized=false at nonce N but never submitted; authorizer later re-authorized and nonce is now N+1; attacker holds the old signed auth. Call sequence: [authorizer re-authorizes at nonce N+1] -> attacker submits old auth with nonce N -> InvalidNonce revert. Invariant tested: stale revocation signatures must not be replayable. Scoped impact: unauthorized revocation of active authorization. Proof idea: fuzz over nonce sequences; assert old nonce signatures are rejected.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setDefaultSettlementFee] Can the feeSetter set defaultSettlementFee for a loanToken that is address(0) or a non-contract address, creating default fee state for a token that can never be used in a valid market, and does this pollute the defaultSettlementFeeCbp mapping without any market creation guard? Preconditions: feeSetter calls setDefaultSettlementFee(address(0), i, fee). Call sequence: setDefaultSettlementFee(address(0), 0, maxSettlementFee(0)) -> defaultSettlementFeeCbp[address(0)][0] = fee. Invariant tested: default fee state must only be set for valid loan tokens. Scoped impact: state inconsistency in defaultSettlementFeeCbp for invalid tokens. Proof idea: unit test asserting setDefaultSettlementFee succeeds for address(0); verify no market can be created with loanToken == address(0).

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the two-step authorization (first authorize ecrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight, then use ecrecoverAuthorizer to authorize a third party) to create a delegation chain where A authorizes ecrecoverAuthorizer, ecrecoverAuthorizer authorizes B, and B can then sign on behalf of A in future ecrecoverAuthorizer calls, creating an unbounded delegation depth? Preconditions: A authorizes ecrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight; ecrecoverAuthorizer is used to authorize B for A; B signs an Authorization claiming to act for A. Call sequence: midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, true, A) -> ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized({authorizer: A, authorized: B}) -> B signs auth for A -> ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized({authorizer: A, ...}, B_sig). Invariant tested: delegation depth must be bounded; transitive authorization must not bypass intent. Scoped impact: gate bypass via delegation chain. Proof idea: multi-step test asserting B can sign on behalf of A after being authorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.touchMarket] Can an attacker create a market for a loanToken where defaultSettlementFeeCbp[loanToken] has been set to maximum values at all 7 breakpoints, and then the feeSetter reduces defaultSettlementFee for that token, causing new markets to have lower fees while the existing market retains maximum fees, creating a permanent fee inconsistency that disadvantages takers in the existing market? Preconditions: feeSetter sets all 7 default breakpoints to max; market A created; feeSetter reduces defaults; market B created. Call sequence: setDefaultSettlementFee(token, i, maxFee) for i in 0..6 -> touchMarket(marketA) -> setDefaultSettlementFee(token, i, 0) for i in 0..6 -> touchMarket(marketB). Invariant tested: market fees are immutable post-creation except via setMarketSettlementFee. Scoped impact: fee inconsistency between markets; takers in market A pay higher fees. Proof idea: differential test asserting marketSettlementFee(idA, i) == maxFee and marketSettlementFee(idB, i) == 0.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.nonce is type(uint256).max, and after successful submission the nonce overflows to 0 on the next increment, allowing replay of nonce-0 signatures? Preconditions: nonce[authorizer] == type(uint256).max - 1; attacker has signed auth with nonce == type(uint256).max - 1. Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth_with_nonce_max_minus_1) -> nonce becomes type(uint256).max -> next call with nonce type(uint256).max -> nonce becomes 0 (overflow in unchecked context). Invariant tested: nonce must never overflow to enable replay. Scoped impact: replay attack on authorization after nonce overflow. Proof idea: fuzz test with nonce near type(uint256).max; check Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic prevents overflow.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketSettlementFee] Can the feeSetter set a market's settlement fee at index i to a value lower than the fee at index i-1 (violating monotonicity), creating a non-monotonic piecewise fee curve where the interpolated fee for some TTM is higher than both adjacent breakpoints, causing takers to pay unexpected fees? Preconditions: market created; feeSetter sets settlementFeeCbp6 (360d) to max and settlementFeeCbp5 (180d) to 0. Call sequence: touchMarket -> setMarketSettlementFee(id, 6, maxFee) -> setMarketSettlementFee(id, 5, 0) -> take with TTM between 180d and 360d. Invariant tested: settlement fee interpolation must be bounded by adjacent breakpoints regardless of breakpoint ordering. Scoped impact: fee accounting inconsistency; takers pay unexpected amounts. Proof idea: fuzz over breakpoint values; assert settlementFee(id, ttm) is bounded by adjacent breakpoints per SettlementFeeBoundaries.spec.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is an authorized delegate for the authorizer use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant themselves (the delegate) additional permissions in Midnight that the authorizer did not intend, by signing an Authorization with authorized == delegate and isAuthorized == true? Preconditions: authorizer has authorized delegate in Midnight; delegate signs Authorization({authorizer: authorizer, authorized: delegate, isAuthorized: true}). Call sequence: midnight.setIsAuthorized(delegate, true, authorizer) -> delegate signs auth -> ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, delegateSig) -> midnight.isAuthorized[authorizer][delegate] = true (already true, no-op). Invariant tested: delegates must not be able to self-extend or escalate their own permissions. Scoped impact: potential privilege escalation via self-authorization through delegate path. Proof idea: unit test asserting delegate cannot grant themselves new permissions not already held.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.claimContinuousFee + Midnight.setMarketContinuousFee] Can the feeSetter set marketState[id].continuousFee to 0 after continuousFeeCredit has accumulated, and then the feeClaimer calls claimContinuousFee to drain continuousFeeCredit, causing marketState[id].totalUnits and withdrawable to decrease below what lenders expect, creating a state inconsistency between lender credit and withdrawable assets? Preconditions: market has active lenders with credit; continuousFeeCredit > 0; feeSetter sets continuousFee to 0; feeClaimer claims all continuousFeeCredit. Call sequence: take (accumulate credit) -> updatePosition (accrue fee to continuousFeeCredit) -> setMarketContinuousFee(id, 0) -> claimContinuousFee(market, continuousFeeCredit, receiver). Invariant tested: claimContinuousFee must not reduce withdrawable below what lenders can legitimately withdraw. Scoped impact: fund freeze for lenders; solvency inconsistency. Proof idea: invariant test asserting withdrawable >= sum of lender credits after fee claim.
